confidence.disclosure: Al-Razi's life is documented by the medical biographer Ibn Abi Usaybi'a and by the philosopher al-Biruni, who compiled a famous catalogue of his ~184 works. Birth and death years are well attested; the chronology of his hospital appointments in Rayy and Baghdad is reasonably secure but not always month-precise. The story of his blindness late in life comes from multiple early sources but the exact year is debated.

sources: Al-Biruni, Risala fi Fihrist Kutub al-Razi
Lenn E. Goodman, 'Al-Razi' in Encyclopedia of Islam (2nd ed.)
Peter Adamson, Philosophy in the Islamic World (2016)

journey.event: Born in the city of Rayy (just south of modern Tehran), then a major Persian centre under Abbasid rule. His full name is Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi.
Studies music, alchemy, and philosophy as a young man. According to later traditions he is a competent lute player and may have made his living briefly as a money-changer before turning to medicine.
Travels to Baghdad to study medicine, possibly under a pupil of Hunayn ibn Ishaq. He observes patients in the new Baghdad bimaristan (hospital) and begins his clinical practice.
Returns to Rayy and is appointed director of its hospital by the local ruler, the Samanid governor Mansur ibn Ishaq, to whom he later dedicates his Kitab al-Mansuri (Liber Almansoris in Latin).

Conducts his famous experiment to choose a site for a new hospital: he hangs strips of meat in different quarters of the city and observes which rot most slowly, then builds the hospital where the air proves cleanest.
Called back to Baghdad by the caliph al-Muktafi to direct the great Mu'tadidi hospital, the leading medical centre of the Abbasid empire. He combines administration with teaching and writing.
Composes his treatise Kitab al-Jadari wa'l-Hasba (On Smallpox and Measles), the first clinical distinction between the two diseases — a landmark in the history of infectious-disease medicine.
Caliph al-Muktafi dies and is succeeded by al-Muqtadir. Al-Razi continues at the hospital but his court patronage becomes less stable.

Returns to Rayy and works for the Buyid prince. He begins assembling the vast medical encyclopaedia that will be published posthumously by his students as Kitab al-Hawi (the Continens of medieval Latin Europe), running to more than twenty volumes.

Writes his philosophical works, including the controversial Doubts about Galen, in which he challenges the authority of the great Greek physician — and, more controversially, his treatises on metaphysics that argue against revealed religion and prophecy.
Goes blind in his last years, reportedly from cataracts. According to one tradition he refuses surgery, saying he has seen enough of the world; according to another the cure is offered too late.

Dies in Rayy and is buried there. His Hawi is later assembled by his students from his case-notes and drafts, and through its 13th-century Latin translation becomes a standard medical text in European universities for four hundred years.
